rationale: The cited paper appears conceptually close to the citing paper’s mechanism-focused account of brand-extension judgments: it centers on “entrenched knowledge structures” and how prior knowledge shapes evaluations of new products, which could plausibly inform mental-boundary/fit reasoning. Both notes emphasize cognitive structure effects on consumer evaluation of novel offerings (brand extensions vs. continuous/discontinuous innovations), suggesting potential substantive theoretical use rather than a purely incidental mention. With only DOI-level match evidence and no in-text context, this pair is still strong enough to retain for full relation judgment.
